mizerMR 0.2.4.0

How mizerMR uses mizer’s extension mechanism

Source: vignettes/extension-mechanism.Rmd
extension-mechanism.Rmd

Overview

This vignette is aimed at developers who want to understand how mizerMR is built on top of mizer’s extension machinery. It assumes you have read vignette("extensions", package = "mizer").

mizerMR uses four of the five extension routes described there:

  1. setComponent() — the multiple resources are stored as a single dynamical component named "MR".
  2. setRateFunction() — encounter and resource-mortality rates are replaced with MR-aware versions.
  3. other_params() — resource-level parameters (kappa, lambda, …) are stored alongside the component parameters.
  4. S3 subclassing — MRMizerParams and MRMizerSim subclasses trigger automatic dispatch to mizerMR methods for plotting, accessors, and projection.

The resource state lives in a setComponent() component

The multiple resources are stored as a single dynamical component named "MR". The component state is an array of dimensions (resource × size) holding the number density of each resource at each size on mizer’s full size grid. setMultipleResources() registers this component:

params <- setComponent(
    params      = params,
    component   = "MR",
    initial_value = initial_resource,   # array (resource × size)
    dynamics_fun  = "mizerMR_dynamics",
    component_params = list(
        rate        = resource_rate,        # array (resource × size)
        capacity    = resource_capacity,    # array (resource × size)
        interaction = resource_interaction  # matrix (species × resource)
    )
)

During a call to project(), mizer automatically calls mizerMR_dynamics() at each time step. That function iterates over resources and applies semi-chemostat dynamics to each row of the state array, using the per-resource mortality computed earlier in the rate pipeline.

The component state at every saved time step ends up in sim@n_other[, "MR"] and is exposed to users via NResource(sim) and finalNResource(sim).

Where parameters are stored

Per-resource scalar parameters (kappa, lambda, r_pp, w_min, w_max) are stored as a data frame at other_params(params)[["MR"]]$resource_params. The per-size arrays derived from those parameters live inside the component’s own component_params, which mizer stores under the same other_params[["MR"]] key:

params@other_params[["MR"]]
  ├── resource_params  # data frame (resource × columns)
  ├── rate             # array (resource × size)
  ├── capacity         # array (resource × size)
  └── interaction      # matrix (species × resource)

Two rate functions replace the defaults

mizerMREncounter

Registered with:

params <- setRateFunction(params, "Encounter", "mizerMREncounter")

Instead of convolving the predation kernel against a single n_pp, it first multiplies the multi-resource state array by the species–resource interaction matrix to form a weighted prey field, adds the fish-to-fish contribution, and then runs the FFT-based convolution against the combined field. It also loops over params@other_encounter to include encounter contributions from any additional registered components — replicating work that the default mizerEncounter() would otherwise do, but which no longer runs because the entire encounter calculation has been replaced.

mizerMRResourceMort

Registered with:

params <- setRateFunction(params, "ResourceMort", "mizerMRResourceMort")

mizer’s default ResourceMort returns a single vector (one value per size bin) representing mortality on n_pp. The replacement returns a matrix (resource × size) by multiplying the predation rate matrix by the species–resource interaction:

mizerMRResourceMort <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, t, pred_rate, ...) {
    t(params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction) %*% pred_rate
}

This matrix is later passed row-by-row to each per-resource dynamics function inside mizerMR_dynamics().

The built-in single resource is silenced 

mizer::initialNResource(params) <- 0         # abundance set to zero
resource_dynamics(params) <- "resource_constant"  # kept at zero forever

S3 subclassing for automatic dispatch

mizerMR defines two formal S4 subclasses so that R’s S3 dispatch routes calls to the right method without any manual if guards:

setClass("MRMizerParams", contains = "MizerParams")
setClass("MRMizerSim",    contains = "MizerSim")

setMultipleResources() returns MRMizerParams

The last action of setMultipleResources() is:

new("MRMizerParams", params)

Because MRMizerParams inherits all slots from MizerParams and adds none of its own, this is a zero-cost wrapping step. Every existing mizer function that accepts a MizerParams object continues to work on MRMizerParams without any changes.

project.MRMizerParams returns MRMizerSim 

project.MRMizerParams <- function(params, ...) {
    sim <- NextMethod()       # calls mizer's project()
    new("MRMizerSim", sim)   # wraps the result
}

This ensures that every simulation started from an MRMizerParams is automatically an MRMizerSim, so the correct downstream methods fire without the user doing anything special.

S3 methods replace manual if guards

Before this refactor, every accessor and plot function contained an explicit check:

if (is.null(getComponent(params, "MR"))) {
    return(mizer::foo(params, ...))   # fall back to mizer
}
# ... MR-aware implementation ...

The same check was repeated in every function, the functions shadowed mizer’s namespace without being formal S3 methods, and there was no guarantee that the two branches stayed consistent.

After the refactor each function is split into a method pair. For example, NResource:

# MR-aware method — no guard needed, called only when sim is MRMizerSim
NResource.MRMizerSim <- function(sim) {
    n_res <- aperm(simplify2array(NOther(sim)[, "MR"]), c(3, 1, 2))
    dimnames(n_res)[[1]] <- dimnames(NOther(sim))[[1]]
    names(dimnames(n_res))[[1]] <- names(dimnames(NOther(sim)))[[1]]
    n_res
}

When a user calls NResource(sim) and sim is an MRMizerSim, R dispatches to NResource.MRMizerSim. When sim is a plain MizerSim (no multiple resources), R dispatches to mizer’s own method. No package-level override is needed and the two branches are cleanly separated.

The full set of S3 methods defined by mizerMR is:

Generic Method class Defined in
project MRMizerParams project_methods.R
resource_params, resource_params<- MRMizerParams resource_params.R
resource_capacity, resource_capacity<- MRMizerParams setMultipleResources.R
resource_rate, resource_rate<- MRMizerParams setMultipleResources.R
resource_interaction, resource_interaction<- MRMizerParams setMultipleResources.R
initialNResource, initialNResource<- MRMizerParams setMultipleResources.R
NResource MRMizerSim NResource.R
finalNResource MRMizerSim NResource.R
getDiet MRMizerParams diet.R
plotDiet MRMizerParams, MRMizerSim diet.R
plotSpectra MRMizerParams, MRMizerSim plotSpectra.R
animateSpectra MRMizerSim animateSpectra.R
plotResourceLevel MRMizerParams, MizerParams plotResource.R
plotResourcePred MRMizerParams, MizerParams plotResource.R

Calling mizer’s method as a subroutine from plotSpectra.MRMizerParams

plotSpectra.MRMizerParams calls mizer’s method internally to obtain the species-only data frame, then augments it with per-resource rows. The internal call must pass two arguments that differ from the user’s intent:

  • resource = FALSE — suppress mizer’s single resource curve (we draw our own)
  • return_data = TRUE — always get the data frame, regardless of what the user requested

NextMethod() would work for return_data: because it is an explicit formal parameter, reassigning it before calling NextMethod() is seen by the next method. But resource is not a formal parameter of plotSpectra.MRMizerParams — it lives in ... — and NextMethod() passes ... from the original call unmodified, so there is no way to inject resource = FALSE that way.

The solution is to call mizer’s method as a plain subroutine, downcasting params to MizerParams so that S3 dispatch selects plotSpectra.MizerParams directly:

df <- mizer::plotSpectra(as(params, "MizerParams"),
                         resource = FALSE, return_data = TRUE, ...)

An alternative would be to promote resource to an explicit formal parameter of plotSpectra.MRMizerParams (with the same default as mizer’s method) and then override both resource and return_data before calling NextMethod(). The as() downcast was chosen because it makes the intent clearer: this is a subroutine call to mizer’s implementation, not a delegation to the next method in the dispatch chain.

MRMizerSim plot methods delegate to the params method

When a plot function is called with a simulation object, it needs to collapse the time-averaged abundances back into a params object and then dispatch to the params method. The MRMizerSim methods do this uniformly:

plotSpectra.MRMizerSim <- function(object, ...) {
    params <- setInitialValues(object@params, object)
    plotSpectra(params, ...)
}

setInitialValues() is a mizer S3 generic with a setInitialValues.MizerParams method. When called with object@params — which is an MRMizerParams — S3 dispatch falls through to setInitialValues.MizerParams via inheritance. That method only modifies existing slots in-place via params@slot[] <- ... and returns params. Because S4 slot assignment preserves the class of the object, the returned value is still an MRMizerParams. The subsequent call to plotSpectra(params, ...) therefore dispatches correctly to plotSpectra.MRMizerParams without any manual re-wrapping.

Summary of benefits

Aspect Before After
Dispatch logic is.null(getComponent(...)) guard in every function R method dispatch — no guards
Namespace Functions shadowed mizer’s generics Proper S3 methods alongside mizer’s
Fallback to mizer Explicit mizer::foo() call inside the guard Automatic — non-MR objects dispatch to mizer
Extensibility Third-party code must call mizerMR::foo() explicitly Standard R generics work transparently
Correctness risk Guard condition must be kept consistent everywhere Enforced by class membership
Bugs fixed plotResourcePred/plotResourceLevel used wrong other_params path Replaced by proper accessor functions

Developed by Gustav Delius, Romain Forestier.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
